name: Xipe Totec
description: Xipe Totec, meaning "Our Lord the Flayed One," was a life-death-rebirth deity in Aztec mythology. He was associated with agriculture, springtime, the east, goldsmiths, disease, and liberation. Xipe Totec was a crucial figure in the agricultural cycle, symbolizing the shedding of the old skin (the dry husk of the corn kernel) to reveal new life within. His iconography is striking: he is typically depicted wearing the flayed skin of a sacrificial victim, representing the new growth emerging from the old. This skin was often painted yellow or gold, further connecting him to the sun and precious metals. He was believed to have originated during a time of drought and famine, voluntarily flaying himself to provide food for humanity. This act of self-sacrifice and renewal made him a powerful symbol of hope and resilience. His priests would also wear the skins of sacrificial victims for twenty days, acting as conduits for Xipe Totec's power and blessings. The festival Tlacaxipehualiztli was dedicated to him, involving gladiatorial combat and the flaying of captives. Through this ritual, the Aztecs sought to ensure a bountiful harvest and continued prosperity. Xipe Totec's influence extended beyond agriculture; he was also considered a patron of goldsmiths, likely due to the association of gold with the sun and the flayed skin’s golden hue. He embodies a complex duality: death and decay leading to rebirth and abundance.
subtitle: Our Lord the Flayed One

keyMyths.title: Xipe Totec's Self-Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Xipe Totec flayed himself to provide food for humanity during a time of famine. This act symbolizes the shedding of the old to make way for the new.

keyMyths.title: The Festival of Tlacaxipehualiztli
keyMyths.description: During Tlacaxipehualiztli, captives were sacrificed and their skins were worn by priests for twenty days. This mimicked Xipe Totec's self-sacrifice and ensured a bountiful harvest.
keyMyths.source: Durán, Diego. The History of the Indies of New Spain

keyMyths.title: Xipe Totec and Goldsmithing
keyMyths.description: Xipe Totec's association with gold stems from the color of the flayed skin, linking him to the sun and precious metals. He became a patron deity for goldsmiths.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. General History of the Things of New Spain

worshipPractices.name: Human Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: Victims, often war captives, were sacrificed to Xipe Totec, their skin flayed and worn by priests. This act symbolized the shedding of the old and the emergence of the new, mirroring the agricultural cycle. The flayed skin, called *teocuitlaquemitl*, was considered sacred and imbued with the deity's power.

worshipPractices.name: Offering of Goods
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, flowers, and precious objects were made to Xipe Totec at his temples. These offerings were intended to appease the god and ensure a bountiful harvest. Goldsmiths, in particular, offered precious metals and crafted items to secure his favor and protection in their craft.

worshipPractices.name: Impersonation
worshipPractices.description: Priests would wear the flayed skin of sacrificial victims for twenty days after the sacrifice. During this period, they impersonated Xipe Totec, embodying his power and acting as intermediaries between the god and the people. This act was seen as a powerful way to invoke the deity's presence and blessings.

festivals.name: Tlacaxipehualiztli
festivals.timing: Second month of the Aztec calendar (roughly February/March)
festivals.description: The most important festival dedicated to Xipe Totec, Tlacaxipehualiztli, meaning "flaying of men," involved the sacrifice of war captives and the wearing of their flayed skins by priests for twenty days. Mock battles, dances, and feasts were also part of the festivities, celebrating the renewal of the earth and the coming of spring.

cultural.modernLegacy: Xipe Totec's influence is primarily felt in academic and artistic circles. He is a subject of study for scholars of Mesoamerican cultures, and his striking imagery inspires contemporary artists and designers. Representations of Xipe Totec can be found in museums and cultural exhibitions, serving as a reminder of the complex religious beliefs of the Aztec civilization.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The exact origins of Xipe Totec are somewhat unclear, but his worship predates the Aztec Empire, with evidence suggesting his presence in earlier Mesoamerican cultures, such as the Yopi people of Guerrero. He was later adopted into the Aztec pantheon, becoming a prominent deity associated with agricultural renewal. While specific myths detailing his birth are scarce, his connection to Tezcatlipoca, particularly the Red Tezcatlipoca (Xipe Totec being a manifestation of him), provides some insight. Some accounts suggest Xipe Totec flayed himself to provide food for humanity, a selfless act that underscores his role as a giver of life. This act of self-sacrifice is central to understanding his character and his association with rebirth and renewal. His emergence represents the cyclical nature of existence, where death is necessary for new life to flourish.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most significant myth associated with Xipe Totec involves his self-flaying. In this story, he peeled off his own skin to provide sustenance for humanity, symbolizing the shedding of old things to make way for the new. This act is directly linked to the agricultural cycle, where the old, dry husk of the corn kernel must be shed for the new seed to sprout. Another important aspect of Xipe Totec's mythology is his connection to warfare and sacrifice. As a god of renewal, he required sacrifice to ensure the continuation of the life cycle. War captives were often sacrificed to him, their flayed skins becoming sacred garments worn by priests. These acts were not seen as mere brutality but as necessary rituals to maintain cosmic balance and ensure a bountiful harvest. He is also associated with the East, one of the cardinal directions, further linking him to new beginnings.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Xipe Totec's worship was characterized by elaborate rituals, most notably the festival of Tlacaxipehualiztli. This festival involved the sacrifice of war captives, whose skins were then worn by priests for twenty days. These priests, embodying Xipe Totec, would go among the people, receiving offerings and dispensing blessings. The wearing of the flayed skin was a powerful symbol of transformation and renewal, representing the deity's ability to shed the old and embrace the new. Temples dedicated to Xipe Totec were adorned with images of the flayed god, and offerings of food, flowers, and precious objects were made to him. Goldsmiths held him in particularly high regard, as he was considered their patron deity, protecting their craft and ensuring their prosperity. The priests who impersonated Xipe Totec were treated with great reverence, as they were seen as living embodiments of the god's power.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: While the Aztec empire fell to the Spanish conquistadors, the memory of Xipe Totec lives on in historical records, archaeological findings, and scholarly studies. His striking imagery, particularly the flayed skin, continues to fascinate and inspire artists and researchers. Xipe Totec serves as a potent symbol of the Aztec understanding of life, death, and rebirth, highlighting their deep connection to the natural world and their belief in the cyclical nature of existence. His association with agriculture and renewal resonates even today, reminding us of the importance of respecting the earth and its resources. Furthermore, the ethical considerations around the practices of human sacrifice in his worship continue to spur debates and discussions regarding the complexities of ancient religious practices and their interpretations in the modern era. He remains an important figure for understanding Aztec cosmology and its lasting impact.

shortDescription: Xipe Totec, "Our Lord the Flayed One," is an Aztec deity of life, death, and rebirth. He's associated with agriculture, spring, and renewal.
